title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on an already installed RHEL 9 system requires adding the dracut fips module and setting the kernel boot parameter fips=1, which is not supported post‑installation and can render the system unbootable or cause cryptographic failures. This change impacts core cryptographic behavior and boot process, posing a high risk of lockout or system breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter modification; potential system unbootability; cryptographic subsystem changes

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding a kernel boot argument (fips=1) and changing the system's cryptographic policy. The description states that enabling FIPS on an already installed system is not supported and may render the system unbootable. This change carries a high risk of boot failure, loss of access, and incompatibility with existing services, thus it is classified as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; potential boot failure; loss of SSH/console access; incompatibility with existing cryptographic libraries; irreversible change on existing installations

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter, which is only supported at installation time. Changing this on an existing system is not supported and can render the system unbootable or cause cryptographic failures, posing a high risk of lockout or instability.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter modification; potential system instability or unbootable state

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: Remediation likely involves upgrading or migrating the operating system to a vendor‑supported release. This is a major change that can affect many services, drivers, and system configuration, creating a risk of loss of SSH/console access or service disruption. Human planning and validation are required before proceeding.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; OS version upgrade; potential service interruption; compatibility with existing applications; possible loss of remote access during reboot

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
triage_reason: The remediation only imports a trusted GPG key and optionally verifies directory permissions. This operation does not affect authentication, networking, or critical boot components and is a standard package management task.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Missing GPG key could allow installation of unverified packages; Potential for incorrect file permissions on /etc/pki/rpm-gpg
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q gnupg; dnf install -y gnupg; curl --fail -s -L -o /tmp/redhat-gpg-key https://access.redhat.com/security/key/567E347AD0044ADE55BA8A5F199E2F91FD431D51
review_feedback: The plan now includes a fingerprint verification step and checks for existing key, gnupg installation, and directory permissions, which addresses the core OpenSCAP requirement. However, it still imports a Red Hat key on Rocky Linux without an OS check, lacks an explicit abort command on fingerprint mismatch, and could tighten TLS handling for the download.
review_concerns: The remediation runs on Rocky Linux, where importing the Red Hat GPG key is unnecessary and may cause confusion.; No explicit abort or exit is defined if the fingerprint verification fails; the plan only describes aborting in prose.; Curl is used without explicit TLS verification flags (e.g., --fail), which could silently accept an invalid response.; The key ID used in rpm -q may not match the full key identifier, potentially causing false negatives.; Potential duplicate imports if the key is present but under a different ID format.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only installs gnupg (if missing), downloads a public GPG key over HTTPS, verifies its fingerprint, imports it into the RPM database, and tightens directory permissions. These actions do not modify running services, authentication mechanisms, or networking. Importing a Red Hat key on Rocky Linux is unnecessary but harmless and will not break critical services.
qa_side_effects: Unnecessary import of Red Hat GPG key on a Rocky Linux system; Potential duplicate import if the key already exists under a different identifier; Installation of gnupg package if not already present

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation only adds the enforce_for_root setting to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, a standard PAM password policy change. It does not modify existing passwords, require service restarts, or affect boot. The change is low‑risk and aligns with typical STIG hardening practices.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; future root password changes may be rejected if they do not meet complexity requirements
review_feedback: The plan will add the required enforce_for_root setting, removing any existing entries to avoid duplication. It backs up the original file and ensures a trailing newline before appending. This will satisfy the OpenSCAP rule and enforce password complexity for root.
review_concerns: If /etc/security/pwquality.conf does not exist, the cp and sed commands will fail, potentially aborting the script.; The backup file is removed at the end of the original script but not in this plan, which may leave unnecessary files.; The inserted line does not explicitly set a value (e.g., enforce_for_root = yes), which might be clearer and more portable.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly backs up the configuration, removes any existing enforce_for_root entries, ensures proper file termination, and appends the required setting. Enabling enforce_for_root only tightens password requirements for the root account and does not alter PAM stack order or break existing authentication mechanisms. The only minor issues are harmless errors if the file does not exist and an extra backup file left behind.
qa_side_effects: If /etc/security/pwquality.conf is missing, the cp and sed commands will emit errors but will not modify other files.; A backup file /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak will remain on disk, consuming minimal space.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: The remediation only edits PAM password quality configuration files to set the minlen parameter, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not modify bootloader, kernel, or filesystem layout, and the change is low‑risk if a reasonable length is chosen.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential lockout if minlen is set excessively high; Incorrect variable substitution could leave the setting empty
remedy_commands_executed: ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-pwquality.conf.bak; which authselect; find /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d -type f -name "*.conf" -exec sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*minlen\s*=.*$/d' {} +; authselect apply-changes; restorecon -R -v /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up existing custom configuration, removes duplicate/minlen entries, creates a dedicated .conf.d file with minlen=12, and applies authselect and SELinux context restoration. It addresses the prior review comments and will enforce the required password length.
review_concerns: The sed command deletes any line containing 'minlen', including commented explanatory lines that might be useful for administrators.; The plan assumes /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d exists and does not create it if missing.; Running 'authselect apply-changes' without first confirming that the system uses authselect could cause an error on systems that rely on traditional PAM configuration files.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly backs up existing configuration, removes duplicate minlen entries, creates a dedicated .conf.d file with minlen=12, and refreshes PAM via authselect. Setting a minimum length of 12 does not invalidate existing passwords, only affects future password changes, so it will not lock out existing SSH or local logins. The commands are safe on standard Rocky/RHEL systems that use authselect and SELinux.
qa_side_effects: Existing users will be forced to use passwords of at least 12 characters when changing passwords, which may cause inconvenience or script failures.; Removal of commented minlen lines eliminates helpful documentation within config files.; If the system does not use authselect, the 'authselect apply-changes' command may fail, though it will not alter the PAM stack.

title: Configure Logind to terminate idle sessions after certain time of inactivity
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/systemd/logind.conf to set StopIdleSessionSec= and restarting the systemd-logind service. This change does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or filesystem layout and is a standard STIG hardening step with minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.
remedy_commands_executed: cp -a /etc/systemd/logind.conf /etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak; install -d /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d; systemctl try-restart systemd-logind; restorecon -v /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d/idle.conf; systemctl daemon-reload
review_feedback: The plan correctly creates a systemd drop‑in with the required [Login] section and StopIdleSessionSec setting, backs up the original file, restores SELinux context, reloads systemd and restarts the logind service. This will remediate the vulnerability on Rocky Linux 10.
review_concerns: The plan does not explicitly verify that the write operation for /etc/systemd/logind.conf.d/idle.conf succeeded before proceeding.; If the drop‑in file already exists with a different value, the plan overwrites it without checking for idempotency.; Using try‑restart may silently do nothing if the service is not running; a normal restart would guarantee the setting is applied.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan adds a standard systemd drop‑in to set StopIdleSessionSec, preserves SELinux context, reloads systemd and safely restarts systemd-logind. This correctly addresses the vulnerability and does not modify other critical services; the only effect is that idle user sessions (including SSH) will be terminated after 10 minutes, which is the intended hardening behavior.
qa_side_effects: Idle user sessions (e.g., SSH, console) will be terminated after 10 minutes of inactivity, potentially causing loss of unsaved work; If a custom logind.conf.d drop‑in already exists, it will be overwritten without warning

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Remediation involves deleting local user accounts. Removing the wrong account can lock out administrators, break SSH access, or disrupt services that rely on specific system users, presenting a clear risk of loss of access.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential loss of administrative/SSH access; Disruption of services that depend on specific system accounts; Compliance impact if authorized accounts are not correctly identified

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: The remediation requires modifying the GRUB2 boot loader configuration, which is a bootloader change. Misconfiguration can prevent the system from booting or lock out console access. Policy explicitly classifies bootloader modifications as too dangerous to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Potential boot failure; Loss of console or remote access; Inability to edit GRUB configuration if misapplied

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: The remediation modifies GRUB2 bootloader configuration to add a password. Bootloader changes are explicitly listed as too dangerous to remediate because an incorrect password can render the system unbootable and cause loss of console access.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; bootloader lockout; inability to boot the system; loss of console access

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing /etc/resolv.conf to add at least two 'nameserver' entries and ensuring the 'dns' keyword is present in /etc/nsswitch.conf. This is a straightforward configuration change with no impact on authentication, boot process, or critical services, and it does not require a reboot.
review_feedback: The plan will add the missing 'dns' keyword to nsswitch.conf and ensure at least two nameserver entries in /etc/resolv.conf, which satisfies the OpenSCAP rule. However, it still edits resolv.conf directly (which may be overwritten by DHCP/NetworkManager) and hard‑codes public Google DNS servers, which may be unsuitable for many environments. Additional checks and a more persistent configuration method would improve the remediation.
review_concerns: Manual edits to /etc/resolv.conf can be overwritten by DHCP clients or NetworkManager, causing the changes to be lost.; Hard‑coding public DNS servers (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4) may be inappropriate for internal or constrained networks.; Adding the 'dns' entry to nsswitch.conf could alter system behavior if it was intentionally disabled.; No verification that the added DNS servers are reachable from the host.; The use of sed -i does not guarantee an atomic write, risking file corruption on interruption.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan makes a backup, adds the missing 'dns' keyword to nsswitch.conf (which is standard) and ensures at least two nameserver lines in /etc/resolv.conf. These changes are unlikely to crash critical services or lock out SSH; they only affect name resolution behavior. The only risks are overwriting DHCP‑managed resolv.conf and introducing public DNS servers that may be unsuitable for a private network, but these are not catastrophic.
qa_side_effects: Potential overwrite of DHCP/NetworkManager‑generated /etc/resolv.conf, causing changes to be lost after a lease renewal; Hard‑coding public DNS servers (8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.8) may be inappropriate for internal networks and could lead to resolution of internal hostnames to incorrect addresses; Adding 'dns' to the hosts line may change name‑resolution order, causing lookups to go to external DNS where they previously did not; Using sed -i without atomic write could corrupt the file if the command is interrupted

title: Disable the use of user namespaces
triage_reason: The remediation involves a simple sysctl configuration change and persistence file edit, which is a standard STIG hardening action. It does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystem layout, and does not require a reboot. The only risk is potential impact on container workloads that rely on user namespaces, which can be mitigated by testing before deployment.
triage_estimated_impact: Potential disruption of container workloads that require user namespaces; Possible incompatibility with applications that depend on user namespace functionality
remedy_commands_executed: ls -1 /etc/sysctl.d; grep -R "user.max_user_namespaces" -n /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; sysctl -w user.max_user_namespaces=0; sysctl --system
review_feedback: The plan correctly disables user namespaces by applying the runtime change, persisting it in a dedicated drop‑in file with proper ownership and permissions, and reloading sysctl. It also addresses previous review comments by handling existing definitions and ensuring immediate effect.
review_concerns: Ensure all commands are executed with appropriate root privileges (e.g., using sudo).; Commenting out existing lines with sed may need careful pattern matching to avoid accidental removal of unrelated content.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly disables unprivileged user namespaces by setting user.max_user_namespaces=0 at runtime and persisting it via a dedicated sysctl drop‑in. This does not affect core system services (sshd, auditd, firewalld, networking) and will not lock out SSH access. The only impact is on workloads that rely on user namespaces (e.g., unprivileged containers, some sandboxed applications), which is expected for this hardening step.
qa_side_effects: Unprivileged container runtimes (podman, docker, buildah) may fail to start containers that require user namespaces; Applications that depend on user namespace isolation (e.g., certain sandboxed tools) may be unable to run

title: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command
triage_reason: The remediation only requires adding a line to a sudoers.d file using visudo, which is a standard, low‑risk configuration change. It does not modify bootloader, partitioning, or FIPS settings, and it does not affect network services. The change is scoped to sudo authorization and SELinux context elevation, which are well‑understood STIG hardening actions.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect sudoers syntax could lock out sudo access; SELinux policy may deny the new role/type if not properly defined
review_feedback: The plan correctly creates a sudoers.d file with the required line to allow SELinux context elevation for wheel members and validates syntax with visudo. It follows standard sudoers file handling and uses safe permissions. Minor improvements are suggested to tighten scope and ensure correct SELinux file labeling.
review_concerns: The rule grants full sudo rights with SELinux context elevation to all wheel members, which may be broader than necessary.; The plan does not explicitly set the SELinux file context (sudoers_t) on the new file, which could cause warnings.; No post‑remediation testing is described to confirm that the context elevation works as expected.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan creates a proper sudoers.d file with the required SELinux context rule, uses the correct permissions, and validates syntax before committing. This is standard practice and will not disrupt critical services or lock out access. The only minor concerns are the broader sudo privileges granted to all wheel members and the lack of explicit SELinux file labeling, which are not likely to cause system outages.
qa_side_effects: Wheel group members gain unrestricted sudo rights with elevated SELinux context (potential over‑privilege).; The new sudoers file may not have the correct SELinux type (sudoers_t), leading to SELinux warnings or denial of the file.

title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a single Postfix configuration line to restrict mail relaying. This is a standard STIG hardening step, unlikely to cause system lockout or critical service failure, and can be validated quickly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; networking affected; Potential temporary mail delivery interruption if Postfix fails to restart; Misconfiguration could block legitimate internal mail
remedy_commands_executed: grep -n "^smtpd_client_restrictions" -n /etc/postfix/main.cf; grep -i "smtpd_client_restrictions" /etc/postfix/main.cf || true; cp /etc/postfix/main.cf /etc/postfix/main.cf.bak; echo "smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject" >> /etc/postfix/main.cf; systemctl reload postfix; systemctl status postfix; systemctl start postfix; postconf smtpd_client_restrictions
review_feedback: The plan correctly modifies the Postfix configuration to set smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject and reloads the service, which will address the unrestricted relay vulnerability.
review_concerns: The plan does not create a backup of /etc/postfix/main.cf before editing, which could complicate rollback if an issue occurs.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets smtpd_client_restrictions to permit_mynetworks,reject, which eliminates unrestricted relay without impacting other system services. The edit is limited to a single configuration line and a reload, which is safe for Postfix. No SSH, audit, firewalld, or networking changes are made.
qa_side_effects: External mail sources that previously relied on open relay will be rejected, potentially causing mail delivery failures for those senders; If the existing main.cf contains complex smtpd_client_restrictions logic, overwriting it may remove intended allow/deny rules

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation only edits NTP/Chrony configuration files to set or add a maxpoll parameter. This is a straightforward text substitution that does not affect authentication, bootloader, or critical filesystem layout, and does not require a reboot. The change is low risk and aligns with standard STIG hardening practices.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; potential misconfiguration of time service; service restart may temporarily lose time sync
remedy_commands_executed: rpm -q chrony; ls -1 /etc/chrony.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -E '/^[[:space:]]*(server|pool|peer)[[:space:]]/ { /maxpoll[[:space:]]/ { s/(maxpoll[[:space:]]+)[0-9]+/\1 10/ } ; /maxpoll[[:space:]]/! s/$/ maxpoll 10/ }' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan will set maxpoll to 10 for server/pool/peer entries and restart the appropriate service, which addresses the vulnerability. However, the sed commands may not add the option to lines that already contain other parameters (e.g., iburst) and there is no backup of configuration files before modification.
review_concerns: Sed patterns may miss server/pool/peer lines that have additional options but no maxpoll, leaving some entries unchanged.; No backup of the original configuration files is made, risking loss of configuration if an error occurs.; The plan does not explicitly exclude commented lines, which could lead to unintended edits if comment syntax is not considered.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies chrony/ntp configuration to enforce a reasonable maxpoll value and restarts the corresponding time service. Adding or updating maxpoll is supported syntax and will not affect SSH, audit, firewalld or networking. While the sed patterns may miss lines that already contain other options (leaving some entries without maxpoll) and no backup is made, these issues are minor and unlikely to cause service failure.
qa_side_effects: Possible temporary loss of accurate time until the service restarts; Potentially incomplete remediation if server/pool/peer lines have other options and miss the added maxpoll; No automatic backup means manual recovery needed if a syntax error occurs

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: Enabling a certmap section in /etc/sssd/sssd.conf is a straightforward authentication configuration change. It does not involve partitioning, bootloader, or FIPS settings, and follows typical STIG hardening patterns. While an incorrect mapping could affect authentication, the risk can be mitigated with backup and testing, so it is considered safe to remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential authentication failure if mapping is incorrect; Service restart may temporarily affect login services; Misconfiguration could lock out users relying on certificate authentication
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up the configuration, verifies a domain exists, adds a certmap section, validates the syntax and restarts SSSD, which should satisfy the requirement to enable certmap. Minor refinements are recommended (e.g., a more specific matchrule, a unique rule name, and idempotent insertion) but the core remediation will work.
review_concerns: The generic matchrule '^.*$' is overly permissive and may not align with security policy.; Using a generic rule name 'default' could conflict with existing sections or naming conventions.; The plan does not check whether the certmap section already exists, risking duplicate entries on re‑run.; Reliance on 'sssd --config-check' may not be available on all systems; using 'sssctl config-check' is more reliable.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan blindly adds a generic certmap rule that matches any SAN and forces certificate extraction for the domain. If the rule is not appropriate for the environment, SSSD may reject logins that do not present a certificate, leading to authentication failures and possible SSH lockout. It also does not check for existing certmap sections, risking duplicate entries. While the backup and config‑check steps mitigate some risk, the core configuration change can break critical authentication services.
qa_side_effects: Potential authentication failures for users without certificates; Possible SSH login lockout; SSSD service restart may fail if the new certmap block is invalid; Duplicate certmap sections on re‑run causing configuration parsing errors
